response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between your .NET 7 project and the WPF libraries, which are designed exclusively for the .NET Framework ecosystem on Windows. When you attempt to run your application on Windows, the runtime expects the specific version of PresentationFramework that belongs to the older .NET Framework 4.0, but your .NET 7 project is targeting the modern .NET runtime that does not include these legacy assemblies by default. This mismatch causes the system to fail in locating the required DLL, resulting in the FileNotFoundException you are seeing.

To resolve this issue without downgrading your main application to .NET Framework, you must restructure your architecture to use a shared class library that contains your business logic and data models, while keeping the UI implementation separate. You should create a new class library project that targets both .NET 7 and .NET Framework 4.8, ensuring that this shared code remains platform-agnostic and does not reference any WPF-specific namespaces directly. By isolating the UI code into separate projects for Windows and Mac, you allow the Windows project to target .NET Framework 4.8 for WPF compatibility while the Mac project targets .NET 7 for Cocoa integration.

cousin projects in your solution structure can be configured to target different frameworks, allowing you to maintain a single codebase for logic while having distinct entry points for each operating system. In this setup, your Windows-specific project will be a .NET Framework 4.8 application that references the shared library and includes the necessary WPF window code, whereas your Mac-specific project will be a .NET 7 application that references the same shared library but implements the Cocoa UI. This approach ensures that the WPF dependencies are only loaded when the application runs on Windows, preventing the assembly loading error on the .NET 7 runtime.

You will need to adjust your project files to explicitly define the target framework for each UI project, using the TargetFramework property to specify net48 for the Windows executable and net7.0 for the Mac executable. The shared library project should use the TargetFrameworks property with a semicolon-separated list like net48;net7.0 to compile once for both environments. This configuration allows the build system to generate the appropriate binaries for each platform, ensuring that the Windows build includes the legacy WPF assemblies while the Mac build remains clean of Windows-specific dependencies.

Finally, ensure that your build configuration in your IDE or CI/CD pipeline correctly selects the appropriate project to run based on the
